response.summary: The slice mostly implements the intended flow, but it does not fully meet the semantic UX contract. The health score area uses implementation-oriented copy instead of explaining user progress, and required detail-view fallbacks include unfinished/generic wording rather than safe, user-ready fallback text.

findings.observed: The dashboard score section is labeled 'Read-only health score' and explained with 'This score comes only from the generated age and gender rules for this profile.' This tells system mechanics instead of what the score means for the user’s progress after completing an item.

Required detail content falls back to generic unfinished-product wording: 'Action details are not available yet.' and 'Timing is being prepared.' The contract requires safe, user-facing fallback copy; these do not help the user understand what to do or why.
The visible score label 'Read-only' is interaction-model language, not user-task language. It weakens the slice goal of showing immediate reflected progress in a clear, meaningful way.
